title: Sum of divisors
description: Compute sum of divisors of n

examples.explanation: n = 6. Initialize sum = 0. Loop i from 1 to 6: i=1: 6 % 1 = 0, so add 1 → sum=1. i=2: 6 % 2 = 0, add 2 → sum=3. i=3: 6 % 3 = 0, add 3 → sum=6. i=4: 6 % 4 = 2 ≠ 0, skip. i=5: 6 % 5 = 1 ≠ 0, skip. i=6: 6 % 6 = 0, add 6 → sum=12. Return 12.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from 1 to n, check if each integer divides n, and sum the divisors. Use this for small n or as a baseline for understanding the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Divisors Using Brute Force O(n)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to compute sum of all divisors of n by checking every integer from 1 to n.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to finding the sum of divisors is the most straightforward method that directly implements the mathematical definition of divisors. Intuitively, a divisor of n is any integer d such that when n is divided by d, the remainder is 0. This approach systematically tests every integer from 1 to n to identify which ones satisfy this condition. The algorithm initializes a sum variable to 0, then iterates through all integers i from 1 to n inclusive. For each i, it checks if n modulo i equals 0 (n % i == 0). If true, i is a divisor, so it adds i to the running sum. After completing the loop, the sum contains the total of all divisors. This method is conceptually simple and serves as an excellent baseline for understanding divisor problems, though it becomes inefficient for large n due to its linear time complexity. The time complexity is O(n) because we perform a constant amount of work for each of n iterations. The space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables regardless of input size. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) by implementing the complete loop structure. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop range (e.g., stopping at n-1 instead of n), forgetting to include n itself as a divisor, or incorrectly handling edge cases like n=0 or n=1. To avoid these, ensure the loop includes both endpoints and test with small values. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def sum_of_divisors(n): sum = 0; for i in range(1, n+1): if n % i == 0: sum += i; return sum.

examples.trace: n = 6. Initialize sum = 0. Loop i from 1 to 6: i=1: 6 % 1 = 0, so add 1 → sum=1. i=2: 6 % 2 = 0, add 2 → sum=3. i=3: 6 % 3 = 0, add 3 → sum=6. i=4: 6 % 4 = 2 ≠ 0, skip. i=5: 6 % 5 = 1 ≠ 0, skip. i=6: 6 % 6 = 0, add 6 → sum=12. Return 12.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Divisor Check Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the loop that iterates from 1 to n and accumulates divisors.
ladders.explanation: The key subroutine in the brute force approach is the loop that traverses all possible divisors from 1 to n. Intuitively, this loop embodies the exhaustive search strategy: since any divisor of n must be at least 1 and at most n (because n divided by 1 is n, and n divided by n is 1), checking every integer in this range guarantees we find all divisors. The algorithm starts by initializing a sum variable to 0 to store the cumulative total. Then, it uses a for-loop with index i ranging from 1 to n inclusive. In each iteration, it checks the condition n % i == 0 using the modulo operator, which computes the remainder of n divided by i. If the remainder is 0, i divides n evenly, so i is added to the sum. After the loop completes, the sum holds the total of all divisors found. This subroutine is the core computational component of the full problem, directly implementing the divisor identification and summation. Its time complexity is O(n) due to the linear iteration, and space complexity is O(1) as it uses only a fixed number of variables. It connects to the core logic (L2) by relying on the modulo operation to test divisibility. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., using range(1, n) which excludes n), not initializing the sum, or misplacing the addition operation outside the conditional. To avoid these, carefully set the loop to include n and ensure addition occurs only when the condition is true. Real code pattern: sum = 0; for i in range(1, n+1): if n % i == 0: sum += i.

examples.trace: Initialize sum=0. i=1: 4 % 1 = 0, sum=1. i=2: 4 % 2 = 0, sum=3. i=3: 4 % 3 = 1 ≠ 0, skip. i=4: 4 % 4 = 0, sum=7. Final sum=7.
Initialize sum=0. i=1: 5 % 1 = 0, sum=1. i=2: 5 % 2 = 1 ≠ 0, skip. i=3: 5 % 3 = 2 ≠ 0, skip. i=4: 5 % 4 = 1 ≠ 0, skip. i=5: 5 % 5 = 0, sum=6. Final sum=6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisibility Check Using Modulo
ladders.desc: Determine if one integer divides another by checking if remainder is zero.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is the divisibility check using the modulo operation. Intuitively, divisibility means that when you divide one number by another, the result is an integer with no remainder. In arithmetic, for two integers a and b (with b ≠ 0), we say b divides a if there exists an integer k such that a = b * k. The modulo operator (%) provides a direct way to test this: compute a % b, which gives the remainder when a is divided by b. If the remainder is 0, then b divides a perfectly, confirming divisibility. This operation is fundamental because it transforms the abstract concept of divisibility into a simple computational check. The algorithm for this sub-problem involves taking two inputs, a and b, and returning True if b divides a (i.e., a % b == 0), otherwise False. This is a constant-time operation, O(1), as it performs a single arithmetic computation, and uses O(1) space. It connects to the building block (L3) by relying on the modulo operation itself. Common mistakes include confusing modulo with division (e.g., using a / b == 0 instead of a % b == 0), not handling b=0 (which would cause division by zero error in modulo), or misinterpreting the result for negative numbers (though in this problem, n is positive). To avoid these, always use % for remainder checks and ensure b is positive and non-zero in this context. Real code pattern: def is_divisible(a, b): return a % b == 0.

examples.trace: Compute 6 % 3: 6 divided by 3 is 2 with remainder 0. Since remainder is 0, return True.
Compute 7 % 3: 7 divided by 3 is 2 with remainder 1. Since remainder is 1 (not 0), return False.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modulo Operation
ladders.desc: Compute the remainder when one integer is divided by another.
ladders.explanation: The modulo operation is a fundamental arithmetic operation that computes the remainder of division between two integers. Intuitively, when you divide a number a by b, you get a quotient (how many times b fits into a) and a remainder (what's left over after fitting as many b's as possible). For example, 7 divided by 3 gives quotient 2 and remainder 1, because 3*2=6 and 7-6=1. The modulo operator, denoted as % in many programming languages, directly yields this remainder. Mathematically, for integers a and b (b > 0), a % b is defined as a - b * floor(a/b), where floor gives the greatest integer less than or equal to a/b. In programming, it's typically implemented to return a non-negative result less than b. This operation is crucial for divisibility checks because if a % b == 0, it means b divides a exactly with no remainder. The algorithm for this sub-problem takes inputs a and b and returns a % b. It has O(1) time complexity as it's a basic arithmetic operation, and O(1) space. It connects to the basic operation (L4) by building upon integer division. Common mistakes include misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers (e.g., -7 % 3 might be 2 or -1 depending on language, but here we assume positive inputs), or confusing it with the division operator (/). To avoid these, remember that modulo gives the remainder, not the quotient, and stick to positive integers as per problem constraints. Real code pattern: def modulo(a, b): return a % b.

examples.trace: Divide 10 by 3: quotient is 3 (since 3*3=9), remainder is 10-9=1. So 10 % 3 = 1.
Divide 8 by 4: quotient is 2 (since 4*2=8), remainder is 8-8=0. So 8 % 4 = 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division
ladders.desc: Divide two integers and get the quotient as an integer.
ladders.explanation: Integer division is the arithmetic operation of dividing one integer by another and discarding any fractional part to yield an integer quotient. Intuitively, it answers the question: 'How many whole times does the divisor fit into the dividend?' For example, 7 divided by 3 gives 2 because 3 fits into 7 two full times (3*2=6), with 1 left over. In programming, this is often performed using the // operator in languages like Python, which floors the result to the nearest integer towards negative infinity for positive numbers. This operation is essential for understanding modulo because modulo can be derived as a - b * (a // b). The algorithm for this sub-problem takes two integers a and b (with b ≠ 0) and returns a // b. It has O(1) time complexity as it's a basic arithmetic computation, and O(1) space. It connects to the concept foundation (L5) by relying on basic arithmetic operations. Common mistakes include confusing integer division with float division (e.g., using / instead of // in Python, which gives a float), or not handling division by zero (which would cause an error). To avoid these, use the correct operator for integer division and ensure the divisor is non-zero. Real code pattern: def integer_division(a, b): return a // b.

examples.trace: Divide 10 by 3: 3 fits into 10 three times (3*3=9), with remainder 1. Since we take the integer quotient, discard remainder: 10 // 3 = 3.
Divide 8 by 2: 2 fits into 8 four times exactly (2*4=8), with no remainder. So 8 // 2 = 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Basic Arithmetic Operations
ladders.desc: Perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division on integers.
ladders.explanation: Basic arithmetic operations are the foundational mathematical operations that form the building blocks for all algorithms in this ladder. Intuitively, these operations allow us to manipulate numbers: addition combines quantities, subtraction finds differences, multiplication scales quantities, and division partitions quantities into equal parts. For integers, these operations yield integer results, with division potentially having a remainder. In the context of the sum of divisors problem, we use addition to accumulate the sum of divisors, multiplication implicitly in divisibility checks (since if b divides a, then a = b * k for some integer k), and division (through modulo) to test divisibility. The algorithm for this sub-problem involves taking two integers a and b and performing one of these operations as specified. For example, to add a and b, return a + b. Each operation has O(1) time complexity as it's a fundamental computation, and O(1) space. This connects upward as all higher levels rely on these operations to implement more complex logic. Common mistakes include overflow in languages with fixed integer sizes (though Python handles arbitrary precision), or incorrect order of operations. To avoid these, ensure you understand operator precedence and use parentheses when needed. Real code pattern: def add(a, b): return a + b; def subtract(a, b): return a - b; def multiply(a, b): return a * b; def divide(a, b): return a / b (for float division) or a // b (for integer division).

examples.trace: Add 3 and 2: 3 + 2 = 5. So output is 5.
Multiply 5 and 3: 5 * 3 = 15. So output is 15.

approaches.name: Optimized Iterative O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Iterate up to sqrt(n), and for each divisor i, add both i and n/i if i divides n, handling perfect squares carefully. This is efficient for most practical cases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Divisors Using Optimized Iterative Approach
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of all divisors of n using O(√n) iteration with perfect square handling.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding all positive divisors of a given integer n and summing them. The naive approach would iterate through all numbers from 1 to n, checking divisibility, which is O(n) and inefficient for large n. The optimized approach leverages the mathematical property that divisors come in pairs (i, n/i). By iterating only up to √n, we can find all divisor pairs efficiently. For each i from 1 to √n, if i divides n, we add i to the sum. If i ≠ n/i (to avoid double-counting perfect squares), we also add n/i. This reduces the time complexity to O(√n) while maintaining O(1) space. The intuition is that for every divisor d less than √n, there exists a corresponding divisor n/d greater than √n, except when d = √n (perfect square case). The algorithm initializes sum = 0, computes sqrt_n = floor(√n), loops i from 1 to sqrt_n, checks if n % i == 0, adds i, and if i != n/i, adds n/i. This approach is optimal for most practical cases as it balances simplicity with performance. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle perfect squares (which would double-count √n), using floating-point sqrt (which can cause precision issues), or not considering edge cases like n=0 or n=1. The connection to higher levels is that this integrates all sub-routines into a complete solution. Real code pattern: def sum_of_divisors(n): if n <= 0: return 0; total = 0; i = 1; while i*i <= n: if n % i == 0: total += i; if i != n//i: total += n//i; i += 1; return total.

examples.trace: n=12, sqrt_n≈3.46, floor to 3. Initialize sum=0. i=1: 12%1=0, add i=1, i≠12/1=12, add 12/1=12, sum=13. i=2: 12%2=0, add i=2, i≠12/2=6, add 6, sum=21. i=3: 12%3=0, add i=3, i≠12/3=4, add 4, sum=28. i=4 exceeds sqrt_n, loop ends. Return 28.

n=16, sqrt_n=4. Initialize sum=0. i=1: 16%1=0, add 1, i≠16/1=16, add 16, sum=17. i=2: 16%2=0, add 2, i≠16/2=8, add 8, sum=27. i=3: 16%3=1≠0, skip. i=4: 16%4=0, add 4, i=16/4=4, so do not add again (perfect square), sum=31. Return 31.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Iterate Up to √n and Find Divisor Pairs
ladders.desc: Implement the loop that finds all divisor pairs by iterating to √n and handling perfect squares.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the optimized approach, responsible for efficiently identifying all divisors of n. The intuition is based on the symmetry of divisors: if i divides n, then n/i also divides n, forming a pair (i, n/i). By iterating i from 1 to √n, we cover all possible i values that could be smaller divisors, and for each, we can compute the larger divisor n/i. This avoids checking numbers greater than √n individually. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty list or sum variable. 2. Compute sqrt_n = floor(√n) using integer sqrt to avoid precision issues. 3. Loop i from 1 to sqrt_n inclusive. 4. For each i, check if n % i == 0 (i divides n). 5. If true, process i and n/i: add i to the result, and if i ≠ n/i, add n/i to avoid double-counting in perfect square cases. 6. Continue the loop. Time complexity is O(√n) for the loop, and space is O(1) if summing directly, or O(√n) if storing divisors. The connection to L0 is that this sub-routine directly implements the main logic for divisor summation. Common mistakes include using floating-point sqrt (use integer sqrt like i*i <= n), forgetting the perfect square check (leading to double addition), or off-by-one errors in the loop range. Real code pattern: def find_divisors(n): divisors = []; i = 1; while i*i <= n: if n % i == 0: divisors.append(i); if i != n//i: divisors.append(n//i); i += 1; return divisors.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divisor Pair Symmetry and √n Bound
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the mathematical insight that divisors come in pairs symmetric around √n.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the key mathematical insight enabling the O(√n) optimization. For any positive integer n, if d is a divisor of n, then n/d is also a divisor. These form pairs (d, n/d). The critical observation is that in each pair, one divisor is ≤ √n and the other is ≥ √n. This is because if both were > √n, their product would exceed n, and if both were < √n, their product would be less than n. Therefore, by iterating through all possible d from 1 to √n, we can find all divisor pairs without checking numbers above √n. The intuition: for each candidate i ≤ √n, if i divides n, we automatically get the pair (i, n/i). The step-by-step logic: 1. Recognize that divisors are paired. 2. Realize that the smaller member of each pair is at most √n. 3. Use this to limit the search space. Time analysis: iterating to √n reduces checks from n to √n, giving O(√n) time. Space is O(1) for counting. Connection to L1: this logic is why the sub-routine iterates only to √n. Common mistakes include not proving the symmetry property or misapplying it to non-integers. Real code pattern: Emphasize the while loop condition i*i <= n to naturally enforce the √n bound.

examples.trace: n=12, √12≈3.46. Divisors: 1,2,3,4,6,12. Pair smaller ≤√n with larger ≥√n: 1 (≤3.46) pairs with 12, 2 pairs with 6, 3 pairs with 4. All pairs found by checking i=1,2,3.
n=16, √16=4. Divisors: 1,2,4,8,16. Pairs: 1-16, 2-8, 4-4 (perfect square, same number). Checking i=1,2,3,4 covers all; i=3 not a divisor.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check Divisibility and Compute Quotient
ladders.desc: Determine if a number divides another and compute the quotient.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two fundamental operations: checking divisibility and performing integer division. Divisibility means that when dividing n by i, the remainder is 0, i.e., n % i == 0. If true, i is a divisor, and the quotient is n/i. The intuition is that in the context of divisors, we need to identify factors and their complements. The step-by-step process: 1. For given n and i, compute remainder = n % i. 2. If remainder == 0, then i divides n. 3. Compute quotient = n // i (integer division to avoid floating-point). 4. Use quotient as the paired divisor. Time complexity per check is O(1), and space is O(1). Connection to L2: this operation is applied for each i in the √n loop to find divisor pairs. Common mistakes include using floating-point division (which can cause precision errors) or confusing / and // in languages like Python. Real code pattern: if n % i == 0: quotient = n // i; process i and quotient.

examples.trace: Check 12 % 3 = 0, so divisible. Compute quotient: 12 // 3 = 4. Thus, 3 and 4 are divisor pairs.
Check 12 % 5 = 2 ≠ 0, so not divisible. No quotient needed.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo and Integer Division
ladders.desc: Perform modulo (%) to find remainder and integer division (//) to compute quotient.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental arithmetic operations used in the algorithm. Modulo operation (a % b) returns the remainder when a is divided by b. For example, 7 % 3 = 1 because 7 divided by 3 gives quotient 2 and remainder 1. Integer division (a // b) returns the floor of the division result, discarding any fractional part. In programming, these operations are typically O(1) time and space. The intuition for their use in divisor problems: modulo checks divisibility (remainder 0 means b divides a), and integer division gives the other divisor when paired. Step-by-step: given a and b, compute remainder = a % b; if remainder == 0, then quotient = a // b. Connection to L3: these operations are the tools for checking divisibility and computing quotients. Common mistakes include using floating-point division (/) when integer is needed, or misunderstanding modulo with negative numbers (stick to positive n here). Real code pattern: remainder = n % i; if remainder == 0: quotient = n // i.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Divisor?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic definition of a divisor and identify divisors of small numbers.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the concept of a divisor (or factor) of an integer. A divisor of n is a positive integer that divides n exactly, i.e., with no remainder. For example, divisors of 6 are 1, 2, 3, and 6 because 6 divided by each gives an integer result. The intuition is that divisors are the building blocks of numbers in multiplication. To find divisors manually, test each integer from 1 to n: if n divided by i has remainder 0, then i is a divisor. This naive approach is O(n) and is the starting point for optimization. Step-by-step: given n, initialize an empty list. For i from 1 to n, check if n % i == 0, if yes, add i to the list. Time complexity is O(n), space O(d) where d is number of divisors. Connection to L4: this uses modulo operation to check divisibility. Common mistakes include forgetting 1 and n as divisors, or including non-integers. Real code pattern: def divisors_naive(n): divs = []; for i in range(1, n+1): if n % i == 0: divs.append(i); return divs.

approaches.name: Prime Factorization Formula O(sqrt(n))
approaches.summary: Factorize n into prime powers, then compute the sum using the formula based on geometric series of each prime factor. This is optimal for repeated queries or when factorization is needed.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Divisors Using Prime Factorization Formula
ladders.desc: Compute sum of all divisors of n using prime factorization and geometric series formula.
ladders.explanation: The sum of divisors problem requires finding all numbers that divide n without remainder and summing them. The naive approach of checking every number up to n is O(n), which is inefficient for large n. The optimal approach uses prime factorization: every integer n can be uniquely expressed as n = p₁^a₁ × p₂^a₂ × ... × pₖ^aₖ where pᵢ are prime factors and aᵢ are their exponents. The key insight is that divisors of n are formed by choosing exponents for each prime factor from 0 to aᵢ. This creates a combinatorial structure where the sum of all divisors equals the product of sums for each prime factor: σ(n) = ∏ᵢ (1 + pᵢ + pᵢ² + ... + pᵢ^{aᵢ}). Each term is a geometric series that simplifies to (pᵢ^{aᵢ+1} - 1)/(pᵢ - 1). The algorithm first factorizes n by iterating from 2 to √n, dividing out prime factors while counting exponents. For each prime factor p with exponent a, compute the geometric series sum using the formula. Multiply these sums together to get the total divisor sum. Special cases: when n=1, the only divisor is 1; when n is prime, the sum is 1+n. Time complexity is O(√n) for factorization, space is O(1). This approach is optimal for single queries and essential for repeated queries when combined with precomputation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Factorize 12. Start with p=2, count=0. While 12%2==0: 12/=2 → 6, count=1. 6%2==0: 6/=2 → 3, count=2. 3%2!=0 → done with p=2. Compute sum for p=2, a=2: (2^(2+1)-1)/(2-1) = (8-1)/1 = 7. Result=7. Step 2: Next p=3, count=0. 3%3==0: 3/=3 → 1, count=1. Compute sum for p=3, a=1: (3^(1+1)-1)/(3-1) = (9-1)/2 = 4. Result=7×4=28. Step 3: n=1 → done. Sum of divisors of 12 is 28. Verification: Divisors of 12 are 1,2,3,4,6,12 → sum=1+2+3+4+6+12=28.

Step 1: Factorize 28. p=2, count=0. 28%2==0: 28/=2 → 14, count=1. 14%2==0: 14/=2 → 7, count=2. 7%2!=0 → done with p=2. Compute sum: (2^(2+1)-1)/(2-1) = (8-1)/1 = 7. Result=7. Step 2: p=3 (skip, 7%3!=0), p=5 (skip), p=7, count=0. 7%7==0: 7/=7 → 1, count=1. Compute sum: (7^(1+1)-1)/(7-1) = (49-1)/6 = 48/6 = 8. Result=7×8=56. Step 3: n=1 → done. Sum of divisors of 28 is 56. Verification: Divisors of 28 are 1,2,4,7,14,28 → sum=1+2+4+7+14+28=56.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prime Factorization with Exponent Counting
ladders.desc: Factorize n into prime powers and store (prime, exponent) pairs.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine decomposes n into its prime factors while counting how many times each prime divides n. The intuition is that any integer n > 1 can be broken down uniquely into prime factors due to the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. We iterate from 2 to √n because any composite factor of n must have a prime factor ≤ √n. For each candidate p, while n is divisible by p, we divide n by p and increment the exponent count for p. After processing all p up to √n, if n > 1, it means the remaining n is a prime number itself (since all smaller factors have been divided out). We add this as a final prime factor with exponent 1. The algorithm efficiently finds all prime factors without checking every number up to n. Time complexity is O(√n) in worst case (when n is prime), space is O(k) where k is number of distinct prime factors, but typically considered O(1) as k is small. This feeds into L0 by providing the (prime, exponent) pairs needed for the geometric series formula. Common mistakes include not handling the remaining prime after the loop, or inefficiently checking even numbers after 2 (optimization: handle p=2 separately, then iterate odd numbers).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty list. n=18. p=2, count=0. While 18%2==0: 18/=2 → 9, count=1. Add (2,1). Step 2: p=3, count=0. While 9%3==0: 9/=3 → 3, count=1; 3%3==0: 3/=3 → 1, count=2. Add (3,2). Step 3: n=1, done. Result: [(2,1), (3,2)].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Geometric Series Sum for Prime Powers
ladders.desc: Compute sum of geometric series 1 + p + p² + ... + p^a for a prime p and exponent a.
ladders.explanation: This is the mathematical core that enables efficient computation of divisor sums. Given a prime factor p with exponent a in n's factorization, all divisors that include p can have p raised to powers 0 through a. The sum of these contributions is 1 + p + p² + ... + p^a, a geometric series with first term 1, common ratio p, and a+1 terms. The closed-form formula is (p^{a+1} - 1)/(p - 1). The intuition: multiply the series by (p-1) and telescoping cancellation yields p^{a+1} - 1. For example, for p=2, a=2: 1+2+4 = (8-1)/(2-1)=7. This formula avoids O(a) addition, giving O(log a) time using exponentiation. In L0, we multiply these sums across all prime factors to get total divisor sum. Key insight: divisors are combinations of prime powers, so total sum is product of these series sums. Common mistakes: using integer division incorrectly (ensure numerator and denominator are integers), overflow with large p^a (use modular exponentiation if needed), and forgetting the +1 in exponent (it's a+1, not a).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute p^(a+1) = 3^(2+1) = 3^3 = 27. Step 2: Numerator = 27 - 1 = 26. Step 3: Denominator = p - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2. Step 4: Sum = 26 / 2 = 13. Verification: Series: 1 + 3 + 9 = 13.
Step 1: Compute p^(a+1) = 5^(1+1) = 5^2 = 25. Step 2: Numerator = 25 - 1 = 24. Step 3: Denominator = 5 - 1 = 4. Step 4: Sum = 24 / 4 = 6. Verification: Series: 1 + 5 = 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Fast Exponentiation (Power Calculation)
ladders.desc: Compute p^e efficiently using exponentiation by squaring.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept computes p raised to power e efficiently, which is needed in the geometric series formula. Naive multiplication takes O(e) time, but exponentiation by squaring reduces it to O(log e). The intuition: use binary representation of e. For example, to compute p^13, note 13 in binary is 1101, so p^13 = p^8 × p^4 × p^1. The algorithm: initialize result=1, base=p, exponent=e. While exponent > 0: if exponent is odd, multiply result by base; square the base; halve the exponent (integer division). This works because p^e = (p^2)^{e/2} when e is even, and p^e = p × (p^2)^{(e-1)/2} when e is odd. Time complexity is O(log e), space O(1). In L2, this computes p^{a+1} quickly. Connection: without this, computing large powers would be inefficient. Common mistakes: not using long integers for large results, incorrect handling of exponent=0 (should return 1), and off-by-one errors in loop conditions.

examples.trace: Step 1: result=1, base=2, exponent=10. Step 2: exponent=10 (even): result=1, base=2^2=4, exponent=5. Step 3: exponent=5 (odd): result=1×4=4, base=4^2=16, exponent=2. Step 4: exponent=2 (even): result=4, base=16^2=256, exponent=1. Step 5: exponent=1 (odd): result=4×256=1024, base=256^2=65536, exponent=0. Step 6: exponent=0 → done. Result=1024.

Step 1: result=1, base=3, exponent=5. Step 2: exponent=5 (odd): result=1×3=3, base=3^2=9, exponent=2. Step 3: exponent=2 (even): result=3, base=9^2=81, exponent=1. Step 4: exponent=1 (odd): result=3×81=243, base=81^2=6561, exponent=0. Step 5: done. Result=243.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Integer Division and Modulo for Factor Checking
ladders.desc: Check if a number divides another using modulo and perform integer division.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation tests divisibility and reduces numbers by division. Given two integers a and b, we say a divides b if b % a == 0, meaning b modulo a equals 0 (no remainder). If divisible, we can update b = b // a using integer division (floor division). The intuition: modulo gives remainder, division gives quotient. In prime factorization (L1), we repeatedly check if p divides n using n % p == 0, and if true, update n = n // p to remove that factor. This is basic arithmetic but critical for algorithm efficiency. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L1: this operation is the inner loop of factorization. Common mistakes: using floating-point division instead of integer division (which can cause precision errors), not updating n correctly in the loop, or confusing modulo with division. Ensure operations work for positive integers.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check 12 % 2 == 0 → true, so 2 divides 12. Step 2: Update n = 12 // 2 = 6. Result: n=6, divisible=true.
Step 1: Check 7 % 3 == 0 → false, so 3 does not divide 7. Step 2: n unchanged, n=7. Result: n=7, divisible=false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Divisibility
ladders.desc: Understand prime numbers and what it means for one number to divide another.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a prime number is a positive integer greater than 1 that has exactly two distinct positive divisors: 1 and itself. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. Numbers with more than two divisors are composite (e.g., 4, 6, 8). The number 1 is neither prime nor composite. Divisibility: for integers a and b, we say a divides b (written a | b) if there exists an integer k such that b = a × k. For example, 3 divides 12 because 12 = 3 × 4. If a does not divide b, then b % a != 0. The intuition: primes are the building blocks of all numbers via multiplication. Every integer can be expressed uniquely as a product of primes (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic). This foundation is essential for L4 and above because prime factorization relies on identifying primes and using divisibility to break down numbers. Time/space: conceptual, no computation. Common mistakes: thinking 1 is prime, or confusing divisibility with other operations. Beginners should practice listing divisors of small numbers to build intuition.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if 6 is prime: divisors are 1,2,3,6 → more than two, so composite. Step 2: Prime factors: 6 = 2 × 3. Step 3: Divisibility: 2 divides 6 because 6 % 2 == 0, 3 divides 6 because 6 % 3 == 0.
Step 1: Check divisors of 7: only 1 and 7. Step 2: Exactly two divisors, so prime. Step 3: Divisibility: only 1 and 7 divide 7. 7 % 2 != 0, so 2 does not divide 7.
